case_name: United India Insurance Co.Ltd vs Minor Mahesh Kanubhai & 2
court: High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad
citation: First Appeal Nos.710-713 of 2007; First Appeal No.3852 of 2008

compensation_breakdown.notes: Tribunal award amount not specified in judgment; insurer had deposited amount earlier; High Court exonerated insurer and directed refund of deposited amounts.

compensation_breakdown.claimant_or_victim: Parvatiben Tejabhai (deceased) - Claim Case No.1208/1996 (First Appeals No.710-713/2007)
compensation_breakdown.notes: Award amount not specified in this judgment; High Court modified award liability to owner and driver and exonerated insurer; deposited sums to be released to insurer.
Individual award figures not stated in this judgment; High Court held insurer not liable where victims were gratuitous passengers and left liability on owner/driver.

facts: These consolidated appeals arise from several Motor Accident Claims Tribunal awards (dates of accidents include 30 September 1996 at village Samadhiyala, Amreli district and 8 February 1999). Accidents involved a tractor (GAO-6007), a Matador Tempo (GJ.5T-2312) and other vehicles; some claimants (e.g. Parvatiben Tejabhai, Chandrakantbhai) died and others suffered injuries. Claimants sued under Section 166; insurers contended policies were 'Act only' and victims were gratuitous passengers not covered by the statutory policy. The Tribunals had in several matters directed insurers to satisfy awards and then recover from owners; the High Court considered whether Sections 147 and 149 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 permit such "pay and recover" directions where victims are gratuitous passengers.
arguments_claimant: Claimants argued Chapter XI (Sections 147, 149) is benevolent and must be construed purposively to protect victims; sub-sections (4) and (5) of Section 149 permit the insurer to be directed to pay an award and recover from the insured and restrictions in a policy (other than those in s.149(2)(b)) are of no effect as to statutory liabilities. They relied on Supreme Court decisions (e.g. Swaran Singh, New India v. Rula, K.M. Poonam, Baljit Kaur) to argue tribunals/high courts consistently follow the pay-and-recover principle even where insurer pleads non-liability.
arguments_respondent: Insurers contended gratuitous passengers in goods carriages are not "third parties" under Section 147 and an 'Act only' policy does not cover such passengers; pay-and-recover directions where no statutory or contractual liability exists are beyond the statutory powers of Claims Tribunals and the High Court and, where given by the Supreme Court, were exercised under Article 142. Reliance was placed on New India Assurance v. Asha Rani, Pushpabai Udeshi, Mallawwa and other authorities interpreting the limited scope of statutory insurance.
ratio_decidendi: “Thus, from a conspectus of the above decisions of the Supreme Court, it is manifest that it is the consistent view of the Supreme Court that a 'third party' policy or an 'Act only' policy does not cover gratuitous passengers in a goods vehicle or any other vehicle and that the expression 'any person' in sub-section (1) of section 147 or section 165 of the Act would not take within its fold gratuitous passengers in a goods vehicle or other vehicle.” “Therefore, in case where there is no statutory liability to cover a particular risk and the policy of insurance also does not cover such risk, the insurance company cannot be made liable to pay the compensation and recover the same from the insured.” “The powers conferred under section 149(4) and (5) of the Act ... are the only source of power insofar as the High Court and the Claims Tribunal are concerned, to direct the insurer to pay the compensation and then recover the same from the insured/owner of the vehicle. If the case does not fall within the ambit of the said provisions, in the absence of any statutory power being vested either in the High Court or in the Claims Tribunal, the question of issuing such directions would not arise.” “Accordingly ... the impugned awards passed by the Claims Tribunal in all the appeals shall stand modified ... it shall be only the driver and the owner of the vehicle who shall be so liable and the insurance company shall stand exonerated from such liability.”
final_order: The impugned awards are modified: the driver and the owner of the vehicle alone are held liable to pay the compensation and the insurance company is exonerated from liability. Amounts deposited by insurers with the Claims Tribunal shall be refunded to the insurers (subject to directions about interim disbursement in First Appeal No.3852/2008).
summary: This High Court appeal consolidated several motor-accident claims where victims were gratuitous passengers in goods vehicles or other vehicles and policies were 'Act only'. The key legal issue was whether Sections 147 and 149 MV Act permit tribunals or the High Court to direct insurers to first pay awards and then recover from owners (the 'pay and recover' doctrine) when victims are not statutory third parties. The Court held that 'Act only' policies do not cover gratuitous passengers and that s.149(4)-(5) apply only to statutory liabilities under s.147; where liability is neither statutory nor in the policy insurers cannot be directed to pay and recover. Practically, the Court exonerated insurers and left liability on owners/drivers, ordering refunds of deposits made by insurers.
embedding_summary: United India Insurance Co. Ltd v. Minor Mahesh Kanubhai & 2, High Court of Gujarat, 07 April 2014, First Appeal Nos.710-713/2007 and 3852/2008. Accidents: 30 Sept 1996 (tractor at Samadhiyala, Amreli) and 8 Feb 1999 (Matador Tempo GJ.5T-2312); vehicles: tractor, Matador (goods/people carriage); deaths (Parvatiben Tejabhai, Chandrakantbhai) and injuries. Core legal issues: interpretation of Section 147 and Section 149 Motor Vehicles Act 1988 (including ss.149(1),(2),(4),(5),(6),(7)); whether 'Act only' (statutory) policies cover gratuitous passengers; scope of the 'pay-and-recover' doctrine and whether Claims Tribunals/High Court can direct insurers to pay awards and recover from owners (versus such directions being imperatively linked to s.149 or to Article 142). Court held gratuitous passengers are not covered by an 'Act only' policy; s.149(4)-(5) operate only for liabilities required to be covered by s.147; where there is no statutory or contractual liability insurer cannot be compelled to pay and recover; insurers exonerated and owner/driver held liable; refunds of deposited sums ordered. Relevant precedents relied on include New India Assurance v. Asha Rani, National Insurance v. Baljit Kaur, National Insurance v. Swaran Singh, United India v. K.M. Poonam, Manager v. Saju P. Paul and others.
legal_principles: A statutory 'Act only' (third-party) policy does not cover gratuitous passengers in goods vehicles or other vehicles unless the statute or the policy expressly provides such cover.

Sections 149(4) and 149(5) MV Act 1988 operate only in respect of liabilities which are required to be covered by a policy under Section 147; restrictions in the policy (other than s.149(2)(b) grounds) cannot limit statutory third-party liabilities.

Claims Tribunals and High Courts lack statutory power to direct insurers to 'pay and recover' where the liability is neither statutory nor covered by the policy; such exceptional equitable directions in past Supreme Court orders are traceable to Article 142.
If insurer pays under s.149 by virtue of statutory operation it may recover from the insured any excess payable as provided by s.149(5) and the proviso to s.149(4).
Beneficial/purposive construction of the Motor Vehicles Act cannot be stretched beyond the scheme of Chapter XI to confer liability where statute and contract do not create it.

bm25_text: United India Insurance Co.Ltd vs Minor Mahesh Kanubhai & 2 High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad 2014 These consolidated appeals arise from several Motor Accident Claims Tribunal awards (dates of accidents include 30 September 1996 at village Samadhiyala, Amreli district and 8 February 1999). Accidents involved a tractor (GAO-6007), a Matador Tempo (GJ.5T-2312) and other vehicles; some claimants (e.g. Parvatiben Tejabhai, Chandrakantbhai) died and others suffered injuries. Claimants sued under Section 166; insurers contended policies were 'Act only' and victims were gratuitous passengers not covered by the statutory policy. The Tribunals had in several matters directed insurers to satisfy awards and then recover from owners; the High Court considered whether Sections 147 and 149 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 permit such "pay and recover" directions where victims are gratuitous passengers. Claimants argued Chapter XI (Sections 147, 149) is benevolent and must be construed purposively to protect victims; sub-sections (4) and (5) of Section 149 permit the insurer to be directed to pay an award and recover from the insured and restrictions in a policy (other than those in s.149(2)(b)) are of no effect as to statutory liabilities. They relied on Supreme Court decisions (e.g. Swaran Singh, New India v. Rula, K.M. Poonam, Baljit Kaur) to argue tribunals/high courts consistently follow the pay-and-recover principle even where insurer pleads non-liability. Insurers contended gratuitous passengers in goods carriages are not "third parties" under Section 147 and an 'Act only' policy does not cover such passengers; pay-and-recover directions where no statutory or contractual liability exists are beyond the statutory powers of Claims Tribunals and the High Court and, where given by the Supreme Court, were exercised under Article 142. Reliance was placed on New India Assurance v. Asha Rani, Pushpabai Udeshi, Mallawwa and other authorities interpreting the limited scope of statutory insurance. “Thus, from a conspectus of the above decisions of the Supreme Court, it is manifest that it is the consistent view of the Supreme Court that a 'third party' policy or an 'Act only' policy does not cover gratuitous passengers in a goods vehicle or any other vehicle and that the expression 'any person' in sub-section (1) of section 147 or section 165 of the Act would not take within its fold gratuitous passengers in a goods vehicle or other vehicle.” “Therefore, in case where there is no statutory liability to cover a particular risk and the policy of insurance also does not cover such risk, the insurance company cannot be made liable to pay the compensation and recover the same from the insured.” “The powers conferred under section 149(4) and (5) of the Act ... are the only source of power insofar as the High Court and the Claims Tribunal are concerned, to direct the insurer to pay the compensation and then recover the same from the insured/owner of the vehicle. If the case does not fall within the ambit of the said provisions, in the absence of any statutory power being vested either in the High Court or in the Claims Tribunal, the question of issuing such directions would not arise.” “Accordingly ... the impugned awards passed by the Claims Tribunal in all the appeals shall stand modified ... it shall be only the driver and the owner of the vehicle who shall be so liable and the insurance company shall stand exonerated from such liability.” The impugned awards are modified: the driver and the owner of the vehicle alone are held liable to pay the compensation and the insurance company is exonerated from liability. Amounts deposited by insurers with the Claims Tribunal shall be refunded to the insurers (subject to directions about interim disbursement in First Appeal No.3852/2008). This High Court appeal consolidated several motor-accident claims where victims were gratuitous passengers in goods vehicles or other vehicles and policies were 'Act only'. The key legal issue was whether Sections 147 and 149 MV Act permit tribunals or the High Court to direct insurers to first pay awards and then recover from owners (the 'pay and recover' doctrine) when victims are not statutory third parties. The Court held that 'Act only' policies do not cover gratuitous passengers and that s.149(4)-(5) apply only to statutory liabilities under s.147; where liability is neither statutory nor in the policy insurers cannot be directed to pay and recover. Practically, the Court exonerated insurers and left liability on owners/drivers, ordering refunds of deposits made by insurers. United India Insurance Co. Ltd v. Minor Mahesh Kanubhai & 2, High Court of Gujarat, 07 April 2014, First Appeal Nos.710-713/2007 and 3852/2008. Accidents: 30 Sept 1996 (tractor at Samadhiyala, Amreli) and 8 Feb 1999 (Matador Tempo GJ.5T-2312); vehicles: tractor, Matador (goods/people carriage); deaths (Parvatiben Tejabhai, Chandrakantbhai) and injuries. Core legal issues: interpretation of Section 147 and Section 149 Motor Vehicles Act 1988 (including ss.149(1),(2),(4),(5),(6),(7)); whether 'Act only' (statutory) policies cover gratuitous passengers; scope of the 'pay-and-recover' doctrine and whether Claims Tribunals/High Court can direct insurers to pay awards and recover from owners (versus such directions being imperatively linked to s.149 or to Article 142). Court held gratuitous passengers are not covered by an 'Act only' policy; s.149(4)-(5) operate only for liabilities required to be covered by s.147; where there is no statutory or contractual liability insurer cannot be compelled to pay and recover; insurers exonerated and owner/driver held liable; refunds of deposited sums ordered. Relevant precedents relied on include New India Assurance v. Asha Rani, National Insurance v. Baljit Kaur, National Insurance v. Swaran Singh, United India v. K.M. Poonam, Manager v. Saju P. Paul and others. Claimant (deceased Chandrakantbhai) - First Appeal No.3852/2008 Rs.4,00,000/- (claimed in petition) Tribunal award amount not specified in judgment; insurer had deposited amount earlier; High Court exonerated insurer and directed refund of deposited amounts. Parvatiben Tejabhai (deceased) - Claim Case No.1208/1996 (First Appeals No.710-713/2007) Award amount not specified in this judgment; High Court modified award liability to owner and driver and exonerated insurer; deposited sums to be released to insurer. Other injured claimants (Claim Cases Nos.1205/1996,1206/1996,1207/1996,1218/1996,1219/1996) Individual award figures not stated in this judgment; High Court held insurer not liable where victims were gratuitous passengers and left liability on owner/driver. A statutory 'Act only' (third-party) policy does not cover gratuitous passengers in goods vehicles or other vehicles unless the statute or the policy expressly provides such cover. Sections 149(4) and 149(5) MV Act 1988 operate only in respect of liabilities which are required to be covered by a policy under Section 147; restrictions in the policy (other than s.149(2)(b) grounds) cannot limit statutory third-party liabilities. Claims Tribunals and High Courts lack statutory power to direct insurers to 'pay and recover' where the liability is neither statutory nor covered by the policy; such exceptional equitable directions in past Supreme Court orders are traceable to Article 142. If insurer pays under s.149 by virtue of statutory operation it may recover from the insured any excess payable as provided by s.149(5) and the proviso to s.149(4). Beneficial/purposive construction of the Motor Vehicles Act cannot be stretched beyond the scheme of Chapter XI to confer liability where statute and contract do not create it.
